wellness_programs.other:
In addition to the programs listed above, our company offers several other wellness initiatives:

1. Bi-annual on-site biometric screenings: A third-party wellness vendor comes on-site twice a year to provide optional biometric screenings, including fasting cholesterol panels, BMI measurements, and waist circumference checks. Employees receive confidential results and can schedule 1:1 consultations with a health coach to review their numbers and set goals.

2. Mental health and stress management workshops: Once per quarter, we host lunch-and-learn sessions on topics like managing workplace stress, building resilience, and recognizing signs of burnout. These sessions are led by licensed counselors from our Employee Assistance Program (EAP) partner. Attendance is voluntary, and recordings are made available for those who cannot attend live.

3. Walking and activity challenges: We run company-wide step challenges two to three times per year, using a wellness app where employees can sync their fitness trackers or phones. Teams are formed across departments to encourage social connection. Small incentives (gift cards, extra casual dress days, and recognition in the company newsletter) are offered for participation and goal completion.

4. Healthy eating and nutrition support: Our wellness committee coordinates monthly "Healthy Lunch" seminars with a registered dietitian who covers topics like meal prepping for busy families, understanding food labels, and managing blood sugar through diet. The company subsidizes healthy catering options during these events. We also maintain a rotating set of healthy snack options in the break room.

5. Ergonomics and workstation assessments: Employees can request an ergonomic assessment of their workstation, including recommendations for chair adjustments, monitor height, and keyboard placement. For employees with documented musculoskeletal concerns, we have provided standing desks or alternative equipment when recommended by a provider and approved by HR.

6. Mindfulness and yoga sessions: During peak busy seasons, we offer short, guided mindfulness sessions twice a week over Zoom, as well as a weekly on-site yoga class held in a conference room during the lunch hour. These are beginner-friendly and focus on gentle stretching and stress reduction.

7. Incentivized annual physicals: Employees who complete an annual physical exam and submit proof (without sharing medical details) receive a small reduction in their employee premium contribution for the following plan year. This is administered through our benefits portal with strict privacy safeguards.

Overall, participation is voluntary, and we emphasize confidentiality and non-retaliation. The wellness initiatives are communicated via company-wide email, our intranet, and during new-hire orientation so that all employees are aware of the available resources.

coordination_of_care.providers_facilities_requiring_coordinat:
1. Dr. Melissa Chang, MD – Rheumatology
   - Practice: Northwest Rheumatology Associates
   - Address: 4521 NE 15th Ave, Suite 300, Portland, OR 97211
   - Phone: (503) 555-2743
   - Reason for coordination: Ongoing management of rheumatoid arthritis for one employee’s spouse who will be transitioning from a different carrier’s PPO plan. The spouse receives regular infusions of a biologic medication (infliximab) at this clinic approximately every 8 weeks. We want to ensure there is no gap in treatment, that prior authorizations are transferred or re-established promptly, and that the rheumatologist remains in-network or that appropriate transition-of-care provisions are applied if not.

2. Providence St. Vincent Medical Center – Oncology Department
   - Address: 9205 SW Barnes Rd, Portland, OR 97225
   - Main Phone: (503) 555-9100 (Oncology Clinic Line)
   - Reason for coordination: One dependent child of an employee is currently in maintenance therapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). The child receives periodic chemotherapy infusions, frequent lab work, and coordinated care from a pediatric oncology team at this facility. It is critical that there be no disruption in scheduled treatments, lab monitoring, or access to the same oncology specialists. We are specifically concerned about continuity of care, coverage for chemotherapy drugs administered in the hospital outpatient setting, and any pre-certification requirements for ongoing treatment plans.

3. Oregon Health & Science University (OHSU) – Adult Congenital Heart Disease Clinic
   - Address: 3181 SW Sam Jackson Park Rd, Portland, OR 97239
   - Phone: (503) 555-6120
   - Reason for coordination: One employee has a complex adult congenital heart condition requiring annual evaluations, echocardiograms, and periodic advanced imaging through this specialty clinic. The employee is established with a cardiologist who has followed them for several years. We would like to confirm whether this clinic and the specific cardiologist will be in-network under the new plan, and, if not, whether a transition-of-care period can be arranged so that the employee can continue seeing this specialist while transitioning to an in-network provider if necessary.

4. Legacy Pain Management Center – Chronic Pain Management
   - Address: 12230 SW Horizon Blvd, Suite 210, Beaverton, OR 97007
   - Phone: (503) 555-4477
   - Reason for coordination: One employee’s domestic partner is under a structured chronic pain management program involving monthly visits, medication monitoring, and periodic interventional procedures (such as epidural steroid injections). The program also requires regular urine drug screenings and coordination with a behavioral health provider. We want to make sure that the pain management contract and current treatment plan can be continued without interruption and that coverage for both the procedures and associated medications is clearly defined under the new plan.

In all of these cases, our main goals are: (1) avoiding gaps in critical or time-sensitive treatment, (2) ensuring that any necessary prior authorizations or referrals are in place as of the new plan’s effective date, and (3) confirming network status or eligibility for transition-of-care exceptions where needed.

specialty_medications.specialty_medications_requiring_prior_au:
Based on information voluntarily shared by a few employees and dependents during our internal benefits meetings, we are aware of the following specialty medications that may require prior authorization under the new plan. Please note that specific dosages and schedules may vary by individual, and we understand that final determinations will be made by the plan and pharmacy benefit manager:

1. Humira (adalimumab)
   - Indication: Rheumatoid arthritis for an employee’s spouse.
   - Administration: Subcutaneous injection every other week.
   - Current situation: The spouse has been stable on Humira for several years under our current carrier, with prior authorization renewed annually. We would like to ensure that there is no interruption in access to this medication when coverage transitions, and that the new plan is aware of the existing diagnosis, previous treatment failures with non-biologic DMARDs, and the critical role this medication plays in maintaining function and preventing flares.

2. Enbrel (etanercept)
   - Indication: Psoriatic arthritis for one employee.
   - Administration: Subcutaneous injection once weekly.
   - Current situation: The employee self-injects at home and obtains the medication through a specialty pharmacy. Under the current plan, they are enrolled in a manufacturer copay assistance program coordinated via the specialty pharmacy. We anticipate that a new prior authorization will be needed and that coordination with a new specialty pharmacy may be required, so we want to make sure that the transition is handled smoothly and that the employee is informed of any changes to pharmacy vendors or copay assistance requirements.

3. Infliximab (Remicade or biosimilar)
   - Indication: Rheumatoid arthritis for an employee’s spouse (same individual noted under coordination of care).
   - Administration: Intravenous infusion approximately every 8 weeks at a rheumatology infusion center.
   - Current situation: This medication is administered in a clinical setting and billed under the medical benefit rather than the pharmacy benefit. We understand that pre-certification or prior authorization is typically required for each course of treatment. We would like confirmation of the process and any documentation needed from the treating rheumatologist to ensure that scheduled infusions immediately after the plan effective date are covered.

4. Imatinib (Gleevec)
   - Indication: Chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) for an employee’s dependent child.
   - Administration: Oral tablet taken daily.
   - Current situation: The child is in a maintenance phase and has been stable on imatinib for some time. The medication is filled through a specialty pharmacy with strict refill and monitoring requirements. We expect a full clinical prior authorization review, and we are concerned about timing so that the child does not miss doses during the transition. We are prepared to assist by providing any necessary contact information for the oncology team and current pharmacy.

5. Dulaglutide (Trulicity) or Similar GLP-1 Agonist
   - Indication: Type 2 diabetes management for one employee.
   - Administration: Weekly subcutaneous injection.
   - Current situation: The employee has had significant improvement in A1C and weight after starting this medication about a year ago. Under the existing plan, this medication required prior authorization demonstrating failure or intolerance of metformin and at least one other oral agent. We anticipate that the new plan may have similar or step-therapy requirements and want to ensure the necessary clinical documentation is available to avoid any disruption.

We understand that the final list of medications requiring prior authorization depends on the plan’s formulary and utilization management criteria. The above list is provided to help flag known high-cost or specialty medications so that prior authorization processes can be initiated as early as possible before or immediately after the new plan’s effective date.
